does: This paper introduces FormalRewardBench, a benchmark for evaluating reward models in formal theorem proving with Lean 4. The benchmark comprises 250 preference pairs where correct proofs are paired with incorrect variants generated through five expert-designed error injection strategies: forced mistakes, minimal single-point variations, verbose incorrect proofs, natural language justification, and Python code injection. The authors evaluate frontier large language models as reward models using this benchmark, addressing the challenge that comparing reward models typically requires expensive reinforcement learning training ablations. By providing a direct evaluation framework, the benchmark enables efficient assessment of whether learned reward models can effectively discriminate proof quality beyond binary correctness signals.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in neural theorem proving that employ reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, where proof assistants provide binary correctness signals for training. The paper responds to a recognized limitation in this line of work: sparse credit assignment from binary rewards that fails to provide learning signals for partial progress on difficult problems. It extends prior work on learned reward models designed to overcome sparse rewards by enabling richer feedback signals. The contribution also connects to broader research on reward model evaluation and preference-based learning in AI systems, adapting these methodologies to the specialized domain of formal mathematics where ground truth verification is available and high-stakes accuracy is required.
enables: This benchmark enables more efficient development and comparison of reward models for theorem proving without requiring expensive full RL training cycles. By providing a standardized evaluation framework, it allows researchers to rapidly iterate on reward model architectures and training approaches, potentially accelerating progress toward more effective neural theorem provers. The work points toward future reward models that can identify and credit subtle forms of progress in proof search, potentially enabling RL systems to learn more efficiently from partial proofs and intermediate states. The benchmark infrastructure could be extended to other formal verification domains, and the preference pair generation strategies could inform similar evaluation frameworks for other mathematical reasoning tasks or code-based formal methods.
